does: This paper introduces LivePI, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate indirect prompt injection (IPI) vulnerabilities in deployed AI agents. The authors construct a structured evaluation framework spanning seven input surfaces, twelve attack/rendering families, and five malicious goals including data exfiltration, unauthorized security changes, and unsafe code execution. They deploy the benchmark on a production-like but controlled virtual machine with live email, chat, web, local-file, repository, and wallet interfaces, then systematically test five state-of-the-art language models (GPT-5.3-Codex, Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Kimi K2.5, and GLM-5). The evaluation reveals attack success rates ranging from 10.7% to 29.6% across models, with group-chat injection identified as a particularly severe attack vector.
connects: This work builds on growing security concerns around AI agent deployment in real-world environments, extending prior research on prompt injection attacks to the indirect setting where malicious instructions arrive through untrusted external data sources rather than direct user input. Previous evaluations of prompt-injection risk have been limited in scope, either purely simulated, small-scale, or focused on narrow communication channels. The paper responds to the need for realistic, comprehensive benchmarking that reflects actual agent deployment scenarios where tools access emails, files, repositories, and web content. It connects to broader research on AI safety, adversarial robustness of language models, and the security implications of tool-use capabilities, while acknowledging that existing threat models have underappreciated the complexity of realistic multi-surface attacks.
enables: This benchmark creates a foundation for systematic improvement of agent robustness against indirect prompt injection, enabling researchers and practitioners to measure progress toward more secure deployment. The structured evaluation framework with multiple input surfaces and attack families facilitates development and testing of mitigation strategies such as input sanitization, detection mechanisms, and architectural defenses. The real-environment testing approach demonstrates a path toward production-relevant security evaluation that could be extended to other AI safety concerns. Future work can leverage LivePI to evaluate new defense mechanisms, study how model behavior differs across architectures, investigate the transferability of attacks, and establish benchmarks for measuring improvements in agent resilience as the field develops more robust safeguards for autonomous systems.
